source_anchors.abstract_scope: rag_survey_source_evidence.md E02 describes hallucination/outdated knowledge, external databases, Naive RAG, Advanced RAG, Modular RAG, and retrieval/generation/augmentation.

bibliographic_record.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Models: A Survey

canonical_facts.objective: Systematically survey Retrieval-Augmented Generation for large language models, including RAG paradigms, core components, evaluation, challenges, and future directions.
canonical_facts.method: The survey organizes RAG research into Naive RAG, Advanced RAG, and Modular RAG, and analyzes retrieval, generation, and augmentation techniques.
canonical_facts.review_scope: The paper reviews over 100 RAG studies and summarizes assessment methods covering 26 tasks and nearly 50 datasets.
key_points: RAG mitigates LLM hallucination, outdated knowledge, and non-transparent reasoning by incorporating external databases.
Naive RAG follows an indexing-retrieval-generation pipeline.
Advanced RAG improves retrieval quality through pre-retrieval and post-retrieval strategies.
Modular RAG adds flexible modules and patterns such as iterative or adaptive retrieval.
RAG evaluation includes tasks, datasets, metrics, benchmarks, and tools, but mature standardized metrics are still developing.
limitations_or_challenges: Long-context LLMs change but do not remove the need for RAG.
Retrieval quality, noisy or irrelevant context, and context compression remain important challenges.
RAG evaluation metrics are not yet mature or fully standardized.
RAG research is expanding toward multimodal domains, which introduces new evaluation and retrieval challenges.
rag_eval_plan_uses: use context relevance, answer faithfulness, and answer relevance as core quality scores
include robustness checks such as noise robustness and negative rejection
compare Naive, Advanced, and Modular RAG configurations
track retrieval, generation, and augmentation components separately
